accepted.question_text: Chaos and turbulence are deterministic dynamical behaviors rather than small stochastic perturbations around a mean signal. What mathematically and computationally effective description of chaotic dynamics, especially turbulence generated by Navier-Stokes flow, can replace Reynolds averaging, avoid the resulting closure problem, and still be useful in engineering practice? More specifically, can one find a description that works better than fractal dimension or SRB measures, and can shadowing or a related method be made practical at the scale of an entire turbulent attractor?

evidence.quote: More specifically, the mechanism of how chaotic dynamics operates is understood; how to effectively describe chaos in term of some sort of averaging is beyond reach (i.e. not tamed); it is not even clear what kind of averaging mean we should be after for!

But with the current computer capacity, shadowing of the entire turbulent attractor is still beyond reach. A description of turbulence which is effective in engineering means a solution of the problem of turbulence.

accepted.question_text: For incompressible fluid dynamics, especially at very large Reynolds number where viscosity is very small, is violent turbulence fundamentally caused by rough dependence on initial data rather than ordinary sensitive dependence on initial data? In this setting, rough dependence means that arbitrarily small perturbations of the initial condition can become substantial essentially instantly; for the Euler equations, at any nonzero time the solution map from initial data to the solution is continuous but nowhere locally uniformly continuous and nowhere differentiable. Does this mechanism, or the very large derivative of the Navier-Stokes solution map in the high-Reynolds-number regime, explain why sufficiently large-Reynolds-number turbulence develops violently in short time?

evidence.quote: My conjecture is that the high Reynolds number violent turbulence is due to such rough dependence on initial data, rather than sensitive dependence on initial data of chaos.

In summary, not large enough Reynolds number turbulence may be due to sensitive dependence on initial data of chaos, while large enough Reynolds number turbulence may be due to rough dependence on initial data.

accepted.question_text: How does chaos theory explain the arrow of time, namely the emergence of macroscopic irreversibility from microscopically time-reversible dynamics? In particular, if a many-body system evolves chaotically so that unavoidable perturbations are amplified and trajectories lose memory of the initial macrostate, can that mechanism account for thermodynamic irreversibility, and in the cosmological setting would the universe still be expanding if the velocity of every object or molecule were simultaneously reversed?

evidence.quote: The arrow of time generally refers to the phenomenon of microscopic time reversibility v.s. macroscopic time irreversibility. That is, macroscopically there is an arrow of time.

For the cosmological arrow of time, one may ask the question: Is the universe still expanding if the velocity of every object (or every molecule) is simultaneously reversed? If the answer is yes, then chaos theory may still be relevant.

accepted.question_text: In predator-prey dynamics, does increasing the prey's nutrient supply or carrying capacity genuinely make extinction of both prey and predator more likely, or is that prediction only an artifact of a particular mathematical model? After rewriting a Rosenzweig-type predator-prey system in dimensionless form with the capacity-predation number H = h/b, where h is the half-saturation prey density for predation and b is the prey carrying capacity, does the apparent paradox disappear because increasing carrying capacity b and decreasing half-saturation h both lower the same governing dimensionless quantity and therefore represent the same effect?

evidence.quote: The most important question is whether or not this paradox can be observed experimentally. It is possible that the paradox is purely an artifact of the mathematical models, while in reality increasing the nutrition to the prey never leads to an extinction.
This offers a resolution to the so-called paradox of enrichment.

accepted.question_text: When a pest has a natural predator, how should pesticide application be timed and scaled so that it suppresses the pest instead of causing a later resurgence above the crop's economic threshold? More generally, what mathematical model captures the fact that pesticide application reduces both pest and predator populations, changes their ratio, and can make a later outbreak worse even when the immediate effect is a drop in pest abundance?

evidence.quote: The paradox of pesticides says that pesticides may dramatically increase the population of a pest when the pest has a natural predator.
To guide the application of pesticide in such a circumstance, a good mathematical model will be important.

accepted.question_text: How can hundreds of phytoplankton species coexist while competing for only one or a few limiting nutrients, even though the principle of competitive exclusion predicts that at equilibrium only one or a few species should remain? Can a nonequilibrium model that combines turbulent water advection, species-specific drift relative to the water, regeneration of the limiting nutrient, and species-dependent nutrient uptake and growth explain sustained coexistence of many plankton species?

evidence.quote: According to the principle of competitive exclusion, the final equilibrium state should be taken over by one or a few species according to the limiting factors. On the other hand, in reality hundreds species of the plankton coexist. This paradoxical question was first raised by Hutchinson [7].

We believe that the essential mechanism of the plankton drifting can be captured by such a modeling. Let f(t, x) be the density of the limiting resource (deficient neutrient), and ρn(t, x) be the densities of different species of plankton, n = 1, 2, · · · , N.
